For the base-case scenario, Aeluma would be a “Buy”, with 44.6% expected five-year CAGR, while for the optimistic scenario, it would be a “Strong Buy”, with 98.5% CAGR. Therefore, I rate it as a “Buy”.

Short-Term Signals

I already talked about their possible financing needs, but I see them more mid-term than short-term (I don’t think they need other financing needs in 2026 because they didn’t reach the commercial stage yet).

The options market can give us interesting hints. For example, an Open Interest Put-Call ratio of 0.28 is considered very bullish, because there are significantly more Call options than Put options, and the ratio is also in a downtrend. However, there are only 4 expiration dates, with one of them very soon in May, therefore I consider this as a weak bullish signal:

And also from the options market, we can observe that higher strike prices have higher IV (Implied Volatility), which is another minor bullish signal:

Aeluma has a high 24% of float shorted, which is not unusual for pre-revenue companies due to their extreme uncertainty. However, I find especially the upward trend as a clear bearish signal:

In conclusion, I see this bearish signal offsetting the bullish signals from the options market.

Risks

I already tried to be a bit on the conservative side for my base-case scenario, with $750M in 2030 revenue, or about 21% out of $3.6B SAM. But let’s suppose now that other materials will quickly advance, I already talked about some of them in the Competition section. Besides, their competitors are companies with much deeper pockets.

With only 10% from that market, for $360M revenues, they would also need to spend more on R&D and S&M to keep up with competition. With 10% net margin (not yet a fully mature margin for calendar 2030) and a P/E=35, a blended target price would be ~$48, only 21% estimated CAGR. That’s good for a moaty semiconductor stock, but not for this pre-commercial extreme uncertainty name.

Despite partnering with Tower Semiconductor and Sumitomo Chemical, the raw materials, gallium and indium, are mostly controlled by China. With the US tariffs war, this can create a serious bottleneck at least for a few years until other supply chains can be developed.

Then, let’s talk about THE risk: since they don’t have commercial partners yet, there is also the possibility (even with lower probability) they will never have one. For example, there are usually differences moving these photonic platforms from a controlled lab to a high-volume commercial foundry. If they cannot reach a certain volume needed for mass markets, one of their primary advantages might fade away.

Even an established partnership can sometimes be lost, if that is with a big company, with in-house capabilities. Take a look, for example, at what just happened to POET after Marvell cancelled all their purchasing orders for Celestial AI.

Therefore, I think it’s normal to assume that, in a pessimistic scenario, Aeluma can go to zero. However, zero means a potential 100% loss. $117 (base-scenario target five years from now) means more than 500% return, a reward/risk ratio higher than 5. Maybe a better comparison would be between the optimistic scenario and the pessimistic one, and we have more than 2900% potential return in five years, or an impressive reward/risk ratio greater than 29.

Strategy

Let’s look at Figure #8 again. With higher IV for higher strike prices, because the options market is bullish, I’d like to do the opposite, sell these higher IV options to collect a fat premium. For a bullish strategy, I need to buy lower strike Call options, and to sell higher strike ones, or to buy Call spreads. For example:

With $1.75 cost/share and $3.25 maximum profit/share, this strategy can bring up to 185% ROI at expiration in November with just a slight movement in the stock price, and it also has a lower break-even, at $16.75. But let’s see some possible scenarios (considering no major fundamental changes):

Scenario #1: ALMU goes above $20. I will close the position when it has very low extrinsic value, for close to maximum ROI. That could happen either with the stock surging way above $20, or very close to expiration.

Scenario #2: ALMU languishes between $15 and $20. I will close it about one-two months before expiration, probably for a profit anyway, since break-even is around $16.75, because the risk is too high to go under $15 for up to 100% loss at expiration. Then, I will just roll it over for another 6-7 months.

Scenario #3: ALMU crosses $15 on its way down. I will close it as soon as possible because the closer to expiration, the higher the loss, for 100% loss at expiration (see Figure #10). However, with 100 days until expiration, for example, the loss would be only ~31% at $15, because our long 15 Call still has plenty of extrinsic value:

Then, I will likely roll over and down the strategy to a 10/15 Call spread, probably targeting again more than 150% maximum ROI, way more than this 31% loss.

Scenario #4: The stock plunges in a single trading session way below $15 (for example, from $17 to $10). This scenario has a lower probability, because it needs to cross $15. If it plunges from, say, $24 to $17, my strategy still holds. However, this can certainly happen for a company in this stage, especially after an earnings report. At $10, with 100 days to expiration, the loss would be ~74%. Of course, we need to double check our targets in this case, depending on the cause of this crash. Considering still no major fundamental changes, I would likely go with an even more bullish strategy, although rolling down to a 5/10 Call spread for up 150% ROI can still give a good overall return.

Dividing 185% from Scenario #1 by 74% from Scenario #4, we have a reward/risk of 2.5/1, but this is just for six-seven months. A stock position has a higher five-year reward/risk, but there is a good probability for this options strategy to outperform a stock position for these six-seven months, with up to 185% with the stock just above $20, while for a stock position I saw 98.5% annual CAGR even in the optimistic scenario.

As for the position size, I generally go with ~0.25x for these extreme uncertainty, pre-commercial companies (which I can increase over time, of course). I am a bit more lured by that huge reward/risk optimistic/pessimistic scenario in this case, but I am combining this powerful leveraged strategy with a few shares:

  • The above Call spread, for 0.15x-0.2x position size, due to its higher probable return for the next six-seven months.
  • A few shares, for another ~0.1x position size, for now.

Takeaway

Aeluma offers disruptive compound semiconductor technologies like InGaAs-on-Si or AlGaAs-on-Si, targeting high-growth markets like defense/mobile/automotive sensors, data centers, and quantum computing.

Aeluma's partnerships with Tower Semiconductor and Sumitomo Chemical enhance scalability, but extreme uncertainty persists due to deeper pocket competitors, supply risks for base materials, and lack of commercial validation.

I rate ALMU as a 'Buy' based on a base-case five-year target price of ~$117, for a 44.6% CAGR, citing a reward/risk ratio above 5:1 (considering it can go to zero in a pessimistic scenario). In an optimistic scenario, I estimate a five-year target price of ~$572, for a 98.5% CAGR, or ~29:1 reward/risk.

I employ a combination of a minor stock position and a bull Call spread strategy, which offers high leverage on the upside (up to 185% ROI in six-seven months) and only minor leverage on the downside if managed early.

data.title: Shell Consolidates Its Upstream And LNG Position With ARC Resources Acquisition
data.description:
Shell acquires ARC Resources

Shell's (SHEL, RYDAF) acquisition of ARC Resources Ltd. (AETUF) is a part of what has arguably been Shell's most important long-term strategic decision in the past decade, namely to bet on becoming a major global player in the LNG space. Looking back to its BG merger a decade ago, which was greeted by the market with considerable skepticism, it may have been the decision that prevented Shell from shrinking in size over the past decade. The ARC acquisition plan is, in my view, another well-timed acquisition that is set to be a small-scale repeat of the BG acquisition.

The implications for ARC Resources shareholders, including yours truly, are straightforward. Shell offers a significant premium over the market cap at which the company has been trading. From my perspective, it is worth keeping the shares through the merger process. It equates to gaining Shell shares at a discount. Furthermore, Shell is set to benefit from what I see as a positive long-term outlook for Shell's external factors, while the ARC merger could improve its long-term internal performance, increasing its global LNG clout, and shoring up its otherwise poor reserves situation in its upstream segment. Taking these factors into consideration, I see Shell as a long-term buy.

Shell stock rating - Upgrading from a hold to a buy

The last time I covered Shell, it was the start of February, before the Iran conflict changed the dynamics of the global energy markets. I upgraded it then from a sell to a hold, on external factors such as Europe's low natural gas inventory levels, which I saw as beneficial to Shell, given its sizable LNG market presence. Shell's share price is up about 15% since I wrote that article, about half of the gain coming since the start of the Iran crisis.

It should be noted that until the acquisition announcement, Shell's share price was outperforming that of ARC Resources by a significant margin. Based on this first glance, one might assume that the acquisition is a negative long-term factor for Shell's prospects going forward. Given the global energy market as I see it going forward, I believe that Shell can use the newly gained assets to its advantage, making it a net positive, which is why I am shifting to a buy.

I bought ARC Resources shares in response to the Iran crisis. I am currently up about 30% on my position as I write this, mostly due to the surge in its share price since the announcement of the merger. Based on the offer of $13.6 billion in cash & shares for the ARC Resources shares, versus its current market cap of just over $13 billion after the initial market reaction, it has little upside left between now and the completion of the transaction. I should note that the $16.4 billion sum includes the takeover of ARC Resources' debt.

If the acquisition fails to materialize for any potential reason, its share price is likely to go back to pre-acquisition announcement levels. The odds of the merger are probably better than 50/50; therefore, I see this stock as a hold, where I intend to ride it out, and potentially gain shares in Shell at the equivalent of about $70/share, versus where it is trading at the moment, namely at about $87/share, given my ARC Resources cost basis.

A look at what ARC Resources can offer Shell, in exchange for the premium that Shell offers

For the full year of 2025, ARC Resources saw an increase in net income of about 14% compared with the prior year, to $1.275 billion. By comparison, Shell saw an increase in earnings of 11% to $17.84 billion for the same period. Based on this metric, both companies have roughly similar momentum, and ARC Resources has the potential to immediately account for about 7% of Shell's earnings.

It is upstream production where the acquisition will help to shore up Shell's outlook. ARC Resources has seen an 8% increase in upstream oil & gas production in 2025 to 374,000 b/d, whereas Shell's upstream segment declined just slightly for the same period. ARC's upstream output is equivalent to about a fifth of Shell's upstream production.

Aside from gaining a sizable immediate boost in upstream output, Shell is gaining important production growth assets that can go a long way in helping keep its overall upstream production from declining in the next decade, as the chart above shows.

In addition to the gains in upstream output, ARC Resources can help to support Shell's growing global LNG footprint. Shell could always purchase the gas it needs in its Canadian LNG plants, but it comes with price fluctuation risks, whereas gaining output can help to cushion those risks.

LNG has been a crucial part of Shell's overall business, as I stated in my prior article. Together with its upstream segment, it provided pretty much all of the earnings that Shell generated in 2025. In this respect, this acquisition resembles the BG merger from nearly a decade ago, which went a long way in propelling Shell into the position of being a global LNG leader. As I pointed out then, the market was greatly underestimating the value of that merger for Shell's long-term prospects.

The market did not see it as a valuable move back then, because at the time, the prospects of Shell and many other major oil & gas companies facing upstream output stagnation were not fully understood. Nor was there a full understanding of the impending failure to break even on many renewable energy projects that Shell and its peers were undertaking at the time. I believe that the market's positive assessment of the ARC Resources deal will not take as long to materialize.

Global energy market outlook for the rest of the decade

When the Iran war started two months ago, and energy prices started to rise soon after, we were offered reassurances that it would be over promptly and that energy prices might even end up retreating to a lower level relative to the market prices we saw before the start of the conflict. We are now nine weeks into the disruption, getting closer to my initial estimate of where we might breach the threshold where we can no longer prevent a sizable demand destruction event, which I stated as being 12 weeks at the start of the conflict.

Based on my prior calculations, a 12-week disruption will cause a loss of about 1 billion barrels in supplies, which I see as being replenished in terms of commercial and emergency government stockpiles around the world within three years, amounting to an increase in global demand of about 1 mb/d for the rest of the decade. Natural gas prices in places like Europe or Japan, which are heavily reliant on LNG imports, are also likely to be higher in the coming years.

Investment implications

Shell's valuation is currently decent at a forward P/E of 8.5.

It trades at a lower valuation level by this metric compared with the largest stock in my portfolio, namely Suncor (SU), which trades at a Forward P/E ratio of about 10. It should be noted, however, that Suncor has an upstream reserve life that is currently about 3X higher compared with Shell's reserves, which stands at 8 years. It is a very relevant metric in my view that, within the current global energy market outlook, leads to an even wider valuation differential in favor of Suncor. It should also lead to Suncor's share price continuing to outperform Shell's, as it has so far this year.

This is the reason why the acquisition of ARC Resources is so important to Shell's long-term prospects. It improves its ability to maintain its current position as a major global upstream producer, within the context of a lack of new conventional oil & gas discoveries. In the absence of such discoveries, companies have to either venture into the unconventional production space or resort to acquisitions. ARC Resources has 2 billion barrels of oil equivalent in proven and probable reserves, which is a significant addition to Shell's potential resources. Going by a UBS estimate, it provides a 33% boost to its 2P +2C reserves, increasing them to 27 billion barrels of oil equivalent.

A chance to greatly increase its LNG exports out of Canada in the longer term, with ARC's natural gas output playing a role in that push, is also a welcome addition to Shell's already formidable LNG position as one of the main players on the global stage. Between the positive and not insignificant additions to Shell's upstream and LNG segments that the acquisition provides it with, as well as the positive external conditions, where higher energy prices are set to prevail for the foreseeable future, Shell is once again a buy in my books. At the very least, I intend to allow for my ARC Resources shares to be swapped for Shell shares as the acquisition proceeds.

Risks to my thesis

As the Iran crisis continues to drag on, we may potentially reach a point where a demand destruction event beyond what is needed to balance the global energy market may occur. At that point, oil & gas producers will probably face a steep selloff. Shell has the added company-specific risks ranging from continued failures on the renewable energy front to the risk of a greater-than-expected decline in upstream production, since it is supported by a low reserve life.

Conclusions

Shell has a current market cap of almost $250 billion; therefore, a $16 billion acquisition may not seem like a game-changing event for the company. A closer look at the details shows us a very different picture. The cited increase in Shell's potential upstream resource base by 33%, as estimated by UBS, is, in my view, the best indication of just how much Shell needs to find opportunities such as ARC Resources that have an outsized positive impact on its poor upstream situation.

The complementary factor of the natural gas resources that ARC Resources provides to Shell's expanding Canadian LNG operations helps to remove the risk of input prices rising in the event that North American natural gas prices start to converge toward the much higher prices we are seeing in Europe & Asia. I believe it is a smart move for the long term because the more we link the markets together through LNG exports, the more likely we are to form one unified market, more like what we are seeing with oil. With these long-term considerations in mind, I intend to initiate a position in Shell by allowing my ARC resources shares to be converted, and I also intend to add to that position on significant pullbacks, which I am sure that we will see this year, especially in the immediate aftermath of a full resolution to the ME crisis.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: American Tower: There's Nothing To Wake The Stock Up (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
American Tower Corporation (AMT) reported its Q1-2026 results yesterday, and the results came in ahead of expectations. This is a REIT that owns cell towers and, to a lesser extent, data centers. I last wrote about this company in January before they announced their Q4 results, so it's time for an update.

I gave it a Buy rating last time, highlighting that its dividend yield was high relative to its history and that the chart was at a support level and could bounce. I also performed a reverse DCF valuation on it and found that the company was reasonably priced.

None of those factors has changed, actually. Further, the company hiked its dividend by over 5% after its Q4 results, so the dividend is even higher now, and the stock is at support again, with a valuation that looks more or less fair.

With its ~4% dividend yield and mid-single digit to high-single digit AFFO growth expected in the future, the stock can be a steady compounder for investors seeking stable companies. And the company's growth will come from secular trends, like a steady increase in demand for data.

But I'm actually going to downgrade it to a Hold for now.

Despite the positives from my last article, I don't see this stock being special enough to be worth owning at current prices, apart from a potential bounce from its support level.

Even though the business itself is solid (I go over the business more in my last article), AMT can continue being a laggard as investors seek high-growth plays.

It doesn't help that AMT has a Sell from Seeking Alpha's quant rating. It has never had a Buy rating on it in the past three years. The quant system was right. Besides decent growth for a REIT and top-tier profitability, AMT's momentum is weak, revisions have been weak as well in recent years, and the valuation is just fair, as I stated earlier. Let's get into it.

American Tower’s Q1 Results

The company beat both AFFO and operating revenue expectations, with Q1 AFFO per share coming in at $2.84, while the consensus was $2.75. Meanwhile, operating revenue came in at $2.74 billion, a modest increase from $2.56 billion a year ago, while the consensus called for $2.65 billion.

The guidance part is interesting because American Tower raised its guidance to adjusted FFO per share of $10.90-11.07, for a midpoint of $10.99. That’s right around the consensus estimate of $10.98. Its total property revenue guidance for 2026 also increased to $10.585-$10.735B, or a midpoint of $10.66B. This was both ahead of the Visible Alpha midpoint estimate of $10.548B and AMT's previous guidance of $10.44-10.59B.

However, a large chunk of the guidance raise was due to FX tailwinds. For example, the 2026 revenue outlook is $145 million higher than it was before, but $110 million of that is due to FX tailwinds, so I wouldn’t praise the guidance hike much. It’s pretty “meh,” if you ask me.

Here are the important numbers to know. The guidance suggests a 3% year-over-year growth rate for property revenue, or 4% growth rate if you exclude the impacts of DISH’s default. And AFFO per share is expected to grow by 2% year-over-year, or, more impressively, 5% year-over-year on an FX-neutral basis if you exclude DISH.

The company also mentioned its longer-term goal to "deliver 200-300 basis points of tower adjusted EBITDA cash margin expansion by 2030," and it plans to invest in AI to expand margins.

The most interesting part of the report was the data center revenue growth. Its Q1 revenue grew to $289 million, compared to $244 million last year, for an 18.4% growth rate or a 16.6% cash revenue growth rate.

Data Center revenue made up just 10.8% of Q1 revenue, but that number is likely going to keep growing going forward, and it should help American Tower maintain its mid-single-digit expected growth rate in the longer term. If the Data Center business keeps growing at this rate for years, then maybe mid-single digits can become high-single digits, but that's uncertain for now.

The Bull Case

1. Elevated Dividend Yield

Bulls will point to the elevated dividend yield. As you can see, it’s basically at an all-time high. Note that the chart below is the TTM yield, but the forward yield is actually over 4% now after the recent dividend hike of 5.3% to $1.79/share quarterly.

According to Seeking Alpha, its forward dividend of 4.08% is 14% lower than the sector median of 4.75%, so if you’re looking for the highest dividend in the sector, this isn’t it.

However, you also need to consider that its dividend per share growth rate (forward-looking) is 5.48%, while the sector median is just 2.68%. This is one of those cases where you’re sacrificing high-yield for a bit of growth, although the 4% yield isn’t bad to start with.

As I argued in my last article, the 4% area has essentially acted as a support level for the stock. Investors have not been willing to let the stock yield more than that for a long time before bidding shares back up.

2. Chart At Support

We find a very similar pattern in the stock as well, which has been supporting around the $170 area for a while now. In my last article, I did argue that the stock could bounce because it was at a support level. It did end up bouncing, but the bounce was only around 10% or so, nothing too crazy. Now, we’re at that support level again, and I would not be surprised to see the stock hold up here.

3. Decent Growth Going Forward

Bulls will also point to the expected mid-single-digit growth that management expects moving forward.

Regarding growth, here's a quote from Rodney Smith, AMT'sExecutive VP, CFO & Treasurer, from the Q1 earnings call:

And we really do feel as though we've moved through a number of event-driven headwinds, not only in the industry for us specifically, and we are moving into a time where we will benefit from the secular technology trends within the sector, that continuation of mobile data capital investment from the carriers, which we still see very stable, strong in that $30 billion to $35 billion range. The carriers continue to roll out their 5G networks kind of at the tail end of that. They'll move into filling in, densifying, increasing capacity across the network. That will all be good for us.

New applications will come down the pike, and some will be driven by AI. And those should all fuel that secular trend of growth, which should be very constructive in terms of supporting us and our business to that mid- to upper single-digit AFFO per share growth.

So, there are real reasons why the company will continue growing. I just don't think it's enough to make this stock a standout in this market.

The Case To Stay Out Of The Stock

1. Valuation Is Just Fair

Just as I did in my last article, I'll value AMT using a reverse DCF model. In the "FCF/share" input of the valuation, I used its TTM AFFO/share of $10.85. I then set the terminal growth rate to 2.5% because that's more or less in line with economic growth. Last time, the cost of equity was 10.15%, but it moved up slightly to 10.38% because the risk-free rate (20-year yield) and the levered beta went up. The levered beta I used for this valuation is 1.09 (per simplywall.st).

Based on those inputs, for American Tower's fair value to be $178.40 (its current share price), its AFFO/share will have to grow by 5.93% annually over the next 10 years and then grow perpetually by 2.5% annually. That's what the market is pricing in at the current valuation.

Last time, the "fair" growth rate I calculated was 5.54%, so expectations have gone up a bit, but it's more or less the same. The chart on the right side of the image shows how its fair value would change based on the growth rates it achieves. For example, if AMT's AFFO/share grows at a 7% CAGR for the next 10 years, the fair value today would be around $192.69/share, or 8% upside from here.

I think growth of around 6% is realistic to expect, meaning the stock is basically fairly valued. Again, nothing stands out.

2. Revisions Have Been Poor Recently

Below is a chart of the FFO/share revision trend for AMT. Besides the uptick it got recently (remember, guidance was mostly raised due to FX tailwinds), the trend has been down. Companies that consistently underwhelm analysts don't tend to perform well, and I'd like to see this trend change. I'm also less confident in future forecasts because of this trend.

3. Stock Has Gone Nowhere For Years

Yes, the stock is at support, so it can bounce. But the medium-term trend is sideways (an up-down chop fest). Even if you adjust for dividends, the stock is at 2019 levels. It has done nothing for almost 7 years. Weak momentum stays weak until something wakes it up.

The Bottom Line On AMT Stock

AMT is a highly profitable company with steady rental income. It's also growing at a modest rate, the chart is at support, and the dividend yield is historically high. A 4% yield plus ~5-7% expected growth can get you returns close to 10% in the longer term, but it's just not enough. And for the time being, momentum is weak, the valuation is just fair, and revision trends are weak as well.

It's "fine," but that's about it.

So, I'm downgrading it to a Hold. AMT needs something to wake it up from its multi-year funk.

data.title: Black Hills: Plug Your Portfolio Into Strong Utility Returns
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

In recent years, the utilities sector has undergone a significant transformation. They have shifted from an abandoned bond proxy to the underlying engine for the AI revolution.

As data center power demand continues to surge and geopolitical tensions keep the interest rate environment elevated, the market is waking up to pure-play regulated utilities that provide a clear growth catalyst.

For income-oriented investors, this environment demands an uncommon combination: the scale required to finance a massive infrastructure build-out and the reliability of a multi-decade dividend streak. One such standout reached a historic milestone earlier this year; we’re referring to Black Hills (BKH), which upped its quarterly dividend per share by 4% to $0.703 in January. That marked the 56th consecutive year of dividend growth for the regulated utility.

When Kody last covered BKH with a "Buy" rating in September, he thought the announced merger with NorthWestern Energy Group (NWE) was a strategically and financially sound move. The investment-grade credit rating was another plus. Finally, shares looked to be deeply undervalued at the time.

Seven months later, we’re reaffirming our "Buy" rating. The regulated utility’s ongoing merger is progressing, with it still on track to close by the end of 2026. BKH maintains a BBB+ S&P credit rating. While shares aren’t the value that they were last September, the stock still looks buyable here.

The Growth Picture Is Intact

On February 4, BKH released its earnings report for the fourth quarter ended December 31, 2025. The company’s total revenue rose by 6.4% year-over-year to $635.5 million during the quarter. For more perspective, that came up $174.4 million short of Seeking Alpha’s analyst consensus in the quarter.

We’re not worried about this topline miss, however. That’s because this topline miss was largely tied to very mild winter weather across BKH’s service territories and regulatory lag.

On the former, the much warmer-than-normal weather across its service territories reduced natural gas consumption for heating. Even though the profit margins remained relatively stable due to fixed-rate structures, this lowered the total dollar amount of sales.

Then, there was the fact that the $350 million Ready Wyoming transmission project was energized on schedule in January 2026. In other words, analysts were simply too aggressive in timing when this new capital investment would start hitting the top line.

BKH recorded $1.41 in adjusted EPS in the fourth quarter, which was up 2.9% over the year-ago period. This matched the analyst consensus for the quarter. New rates/rider recovery and customer growth more than offset headwinds from higher financing costs and greater depreciation and amortization on its larger rate base.

BKH reports its Q1 earnings on May 6, analysts predict an EPS of $1.84. It's important to remember that BKH suffers from seasonality, meaning that its Q1 and Q4 earnings are always the strongest of its entire year, largely due to the colder weather that is endured during those six months. In the spring and summer, typically, heating and energy demand are lower, which leads to reduced earnings. Last quarter saw slightly less than expected revenue because of warmer-than-expected weather. Warmer-than-normal weather has persisted in Colorado, especially, which may lead to a decline in expected revenue, even if earnings remain as expected.

Since Kody already laid out the benefits of the pending merger with NWE in his prior article, we won’t go into those details again. As we alluded to earlier, one forward-looking catalyst for BKH will come from rider recovery on strategic investments, such as Ready Wyoming and Lange II.

Then there is the rate case front for the company. In her opening remarks during the Q4 2025 Earnings Call, Chief Utility Officer Marne Jones noted that BKH filed a new rate review for Arkansas Gas, seeking a recovery of $147 million of new investments since its last review in 2023. The company is requesting $29.4 million in new annual revenue at a return on equity of 10.5% at a roughly 50/50 capital structure. These new rates are anticipated in the second half of 2026.

In February, the company’s electric utility filed a rate review application with the South Dakota Public Utilities Commission. This is seeking $50.6 million in new annual revenue for the recovery of $523 million of investments since its last rate review in 2014. New rates are expected to be finalized in the first quarter of 2027.

BKH had a very productive year for growing its data center pipeline. In 2025 alone, this soared from over 1 gigawatt of load requests to more than 3 GW. The company is benefiting from Meta Platforms (META) ramping up its new data center and continued growth from Microsoft (MSFT). By 2028, data center demand is expected to contribute to 10%+ of its growing adjusted EPS base.

BKH also has the financial means to keep up with growing customer demand. That’s because its long-term FFO-to-debt target of 14% to 15% is comfortably above the downgrade threshold of 13%. BKH’s net debt to capitalization ratio of around 55% is healthy for a regulated utility. This is what supports the BBB+ S&P credit rating with a stable outlook and the Baa2 (BBB equivalent) Moody’s credit rating with a stable outlook.

For these reasons, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for adjusted EPS to grow by 6.1% in 2026 to $4.35. For 2027, another 5.8% increase in adjusted EPS to $4.60 is anticipated. In 2028, an additional 7.4% jump in adjusted EPS to $4.94 is the current consensus (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead was according to BKH’s Q4 2025 Earnings Press Release and BKH’s Q4 2025 Earnings Presentation).

Fair Value Is Above $80 A Share

We think that it still has some upside left.

At the current $75 share price (as of April 27, 2026), the stock is trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 17. This is just below its 10-year average P/E ratio of 17.3, according to FAST Graphs.

Moving forward, we would argue that a valuation multiple of roughly one turn higher than the 10-year average (18.5) can be defended. That’s because BKH’s 6.4% annual forward adjusted EPS growth consensus comes in at double the 10-year CAGR of 3.2%.

Upon the closing of its merger with NWE, this level of growth will be cemented for the foreseeable future. This slight boost to an already solid growth profile could be enough to more than counter the elevated interest rate environment.

The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield of 4.3% (US10Y) is well above the 10-year average yield of 2.7%, per Macrotrends. We believe that the 10-year yield will remain in the low-4% range in the years ahead.

Over the past 10 years, BKH’s earnings yield spread to the 10-year yield has been roughly 300 basis points. In the coming years, we are forecasting a roughly 125 basis point spread between the two. This is backed up by the bolstered growth prospects.

Applying our fair value PE ratio of 18.5, we arrive at a fair value of $82 a share.

If BKH matches growth projections and reverts to fair value, it could generate an 13% total return through March 2027. By the end of 2031, BKH could post 9.95% annual total returns.

Steady Payout Growth Lies Ahead

BKH’s 3.7% forward dividend yield clocks in roughly 60 basis points above the sector median forward yield of 3.1%. This represents a 40-basis-point narrowing of the spread since September. That’s sufficient for BKH to earn respective B+ grades from Seeking Alpha’s Quant System for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield (both of which are unchanged from last September).

The regulated utility has earned a reputation for reliable dividend growth over the decades. Its 56-year dividend growth streak sits well above the sector median of just 9.1 years. This is why the Quant System issues an A+ grade for overall dividend consistency (the same as it was in September).

BKH looks well-positioned to build on its illustrious dividend growth streak as well. That’s evidenced by an adjusted EPS payout ratio set to be in the mid-60% range for 2026 (on the high end of its targeted range between 55% and 65%). Per the Zen Research Terminal, this provides a buffer relative to the 75% payout ratio that rating agencies prefer from the industry.

That’s why the Zen Research Terminal assigns a 1% probability of a payout reduction within the next 12 months. In the next average recession, this rises to 2.5%. Consequently, BKH garners a B+ grade from the Quant System for overall dividend safety (unchanged from September).

The Quant System anticipates 4% forward dividend per share growth. That would be moderately below the sector median of 5.3%. Overall, we think that this is a reasonable expectation for dividend growth. This would allow BKH to reduce the payout ratio more toward the lower end of its targeted payout ratio over the next several years. From there, we could see more solid mid-single-digit percentage dividend growth. That accounts for the Quant System’s C- grade for forward dividend per share growth (the same as it was in September).

Risks To Consider

BKH is a regulated utility with a bright future ahead. Still, it faces risks that could alter or even break the investment thesis if they materialize.

On the one hand, there’s the news that the pending merger with NWE was approved by both companies’ shareholders. This puts the combined company one step closer to fruition, which will be called Bright Horizon Energy Corporation.

Still, the company must secure approvals from several state regulators, such as Montana’s Public Service Commission, as well as from Nebraska and South Dakota’s state regulators, and the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission.

Perhaps the biggest concern will be in Montana. Advocacy groups, including the Montana Environmental Information Center and the Montana Consumer Counsel, have expressed concern that the megamerger could lead to higher utility bills. This is because it’s feared that NWE’s already huge influence in Montana politics could be amplified, making it harder for local regulators to hold it accountable.

The combined company may have to commit to a rate freeze for a few years following the close of the merger. It may also have to issue a one-time or recurring merger credit to customers, funded by the merger synergies.

This risk is especially worth noting because, around the country, older citizens are becoming more concerned with the rising costs of utility services. An AARP survey of more than 1,000 Americans aged 50 and older last April found that three in four surveyed were worried about their utilities going up. Of that group, 83% feared an increase in the cost of their electricity service. This group tends to be the most influential voting bloc, so they hold the ability to put pressure on state regulators.

Another risk to BKH is that its status as a major regulated utility puts it in the crosshairs of Iranian-affiliated cyber actors. Recently, several U.S. government agencies issued a joint advisory warning that Iranian-linked groups are actively targeting Programmable Logic Controllers. These are the industrial computers that manage power grids and water systems. If any major cyber breaches occur, that could cost BKH via operational downtime and resulting litigation.

A Quality Utility Worth Considering

While the ongoing merger with NWE still faces some hurdles, I’m confident that it will be finalized with some concessions. That could easily push adjusted EPS growth north of 6% annually. BKH’s BBB+ S&P credit rating provides it with the low cost of capital to fund its capex as well. The dividend is also poised to keep growing.

Shares are also marginally undervalued. This could provide 10% annual total returns, which is enough from the Dividend King for us to stick with our "Buy" rating.

data.title: SCHY: Diversify Your Dividend Portfolio With One Click
data.description:
The Schwab International Dividend Equity ETF (SCHY) offers U.S. investors the opportunity to diversify away from U.S. companies in an easy and cost-efficient way, which has been a winning strategy over the last year. I believe this outperformance continues. Here's why.

Introduction

When I last wrote about SCHY back in 2024, my thesis was that U.S. stocks are mostly overvalued and diversifying into other markets wasn't such a bad idea. This rotation would help drive outperformance for international stocks. And since I'm a dividend investor, I was attracted to the then-4%+ yield offered while I waited for things to play out.

One part of my thesis was correct, while the other part didn't exactly materialize. SCHY (and international stocks more broadly) have outperformed the S&P 500, delivering a total return of more than 40% since the article was written in November 2024, versus a 20.75% return for the S&P 500. Investors moved into international stocks, but the S&P 500 has still performed relatively well.

There are still signs that broad U.S. stock markets are overvalued. The Shiller P/E ratio (or the cyclically adjusted price-earnings ratio, better known as CAPE) continues to show a market that trades at a elevated valuation. CAPE is more than 40x, which is officially the second-highest level ever. Only the tech bubble was more expensive on a CAPE basis.

The S&P 500 dividend yield has slumped all the way down to 1.09%. This is the lowest dividend yield ever recorded; 2000 only saw the S&P 500's dividend yield fall to 1.13%. Sure, there are arguments against using dividend yield as a proxy of value, especially in a world where companies will often choose the flexibility of buybacks instead. But this analyst thinks it still has some merit.

Saying that, I'm the first to admit the predicting bubbles business is a hard one, and most investors shouldn't even try. Both of these metrics have indicated U.S. stocks have been overvalued for at least the last decade, and indexes have continued their almost uninterrupted march higher.

We must also remember that indexes like the S&P 500 or NASDAQ 100 don't represent the entire market. There's value to be found if we look under the surface. If investor cash is flooding into passive broad market ETFs and sexy tech and AI names, then it stands to reason that there are bargains to be found in the boring world of dividends. The bargains can get even deeper if investors are convinced a company is about to be damaged by some new revolutionary technology.

Because of this, I no longer think it's a good idea to invest in something like SCHY because you're worried about a bubble. The better reason is to diversify your income outside of the U.S. market, a move that has multiple advantages. Let's take a closer look at a few of those advantages.

Why Bother?

I'm a Canadian dividend investor who, for a long time, had virtually all my dividend income coming to me in Canadian dollars.

The logic was fairly simple. My expenses are in local currency. I understood the Canadian market well, and so I invested in companies that I was familiar with. Plus, Canadian dividends are taxed extremely well when they are your only source of income. My goal was to retire on Canadian dividends partly because of that tax treatment.

But I've since changed my mind a little bit, and have added dividend stocks to the portfolio outside of Canada. I've purchased shares of U.S. stocks, but also expanded into companies with operations solely in Europe or Latin America.

I did this for a few reasons. The main one is that my TSX-centric dividend portfolio had a lot of Canada exposure. If the Canadian economy stumbles —as it often does when oil and other natural resource prices are weak — my entire portfolio takes a hit.

But there are other reasons. I like to travel, and I spend at least a few weeks per year outside of Canada. These are heavy spending weeks compared to normal weeks back home. When the Canadian dollar is weak compared to other currencies, that spending takes a bigger chunk out of my budget. Therefore, I believe it makes sense to add in some foreign exposure as a hedge on that future spending.

Even if you're not a traveler, it still makes sense to diversify outside of the U.S. Sometimes the U.S. dollar is strong, which impacts earnings from foreign subsidiaries when converted back to local currency. It also makes it harder for exporters to make a buck. A weak U.S. dollar helps foreign operations, which is a tailwind for earnings as that capital is brought back home.

By having some of their portfolio in U.S. dollar names and some more internationally, an investor can smooth out some of those effects. Such a portfolio would also take advantage of bull markets in foreign stocks, just like what has happened in the last 18 months or so.

Why SCHY?

This begs the question: why should an investor use SCHY specifically to implement such a strategy?

There are a few reasons why this particular Schwab ETF is an attractive choice for such an exercise. The first is the instant diversification it offers. With just one click of a mouse, an investor gets exposure to 138 different foreign companies.

The top holdings include a diverse mix of companies from various European countries and differing sectors. Here's a glimpse of the top ten holdings.

The top holdings don't have much correlation to the U.S. market, which helps make SCHY an ideal hedge for those looking to get away from domestic equities. For instance, according to a recent fact sheet, Allianz SE (ALIZY) only gets 14% of its revenue from the United States, versus more than 50% in Europe. Less than 15% of Enel SpA's (ENLAY) power generation comes from Canada and the United States. BHP Group Limited (BHP) is a diversified miner with operations around the world. These are not companies with huge U.S. exposure.

SCHY is a heavily European fund, but it has exposure to markets outside of the continent as well. Australian companies make up nearly 10% of the fund's assets, and it has about 5% of assets invested in Indian companies and a little more than 4% of assets invested in companies from Singapore.

It also added companies from Saudi Arabia, South Africa, Kuwait, and Thailand in its latest reconstitution, and the fund has had exposure to companies in other diverse markets such as Brazil, Canada, Indonesia, South Korea, and Taiwan in the past. Some of these companies no longer meet SCHY's investment criteria, and so they get punted in the annual reconstitution.

Here's a summary of this year's reconstitution, which happened in March.

The portfolio is well diversified, with exposure to most sectors. It is more heavily weighted towards financials and communication services, with each around 15% of assets. Consumer staples and industrials aren't far behind. I will note the ETF doesn't have much exposure to tech; only about 4% of the portfolio is invested in tech companies.

SCHY follows the Dow Jones International Dividend 100 Index, which focuses on the sustainability and quality of the underlying dividend by measuring four distinct quality attributes. They include:

  • Balance sheet cleanliness (measured by free cash flow/debt)
  • Operational efficiency (measured by return on equity
  • Balance of yield and growth (measured by a combination of dividend yield and five-year dividend growth rate)

These are the exact same attributes that the index uses to measure the quality of U.S. dividend names for its more famous ETF, the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD).

The objective of the reconstitution is to improve the quality of the portfolio. The index kicks out companies that no longer offer the qualities it is looking for, and replaces them with a new batch of companies that do comply.

The upside is such a move generally helps dividend growth, since lower yielders are often replaced with companies that offer a higher yield. The downside is something I explored when I last wrote about SCHD: the index will sometimes kick out winners much too soon.

Finally, I'll mention one of the bigger advantages of SCHY — the miniscule 0.08% expense ratio. This expense ratio is so cheap that it makes sense for individual investors to just not bother researching individual foreign stocks. They can outsource that management at the low cost of 80 cents for every $1,000 invested.

SCHY's valuation

Another reason to be bullish on SCHY is even after it has moved up significantly, the ETF is still quite reasonably valued. Especially when we compare it to the U.S. market.

As of March 31st, SCHY traded for just over 15x earnings, a hair over 8x cash flow, and offered a return on equity of more than 21%.

One advantage to SCHY's reconstitution process is the ETF is constantly rotating out what the index determines to be expensive and lower quality stocks in favor of more reasonably valued names. As long as SCHY continues this process, it will likely always trade at a reasonable valuation.

The process has another benefit for dividend investors. Remember, the dividend yield of an underlying holding is a consideration of whether it remains in the ETF or not. The reconstitution process essentially sells a bunch of lower yielding stocks in favor of ones with higher yields.

By instituting a rule to look for higher yields before it looks for lower yields, SCHY is essentially a value fund. A higher than normal dividend yield is a reasonable proxy for value.

Risks

One factor helping SCHY today is that European equities are back in favor. But this sentiment could easily change.

For example, we're seeing reports that the Iran war is having an impact on European energy supplies. Much of Europe's oil is imported from the Middle East, and issues with the Strait of Hormuz could have a big impact on the continent's economy. Yes, SCHY does hold energy stocks (the fund has about a 10% energy weighting today) but it's unlikely to be enough exposure to offset a major energy crisis.

The other risk to SCHY is tech continuing to outperform. This is an ETF with less than 5% of its assets invested in technology companies. If U.S. tech continues to zoom higher, it's likely this fund will be left behind.

The Bottom Line

SCHY is an excellent way for U.S. dividend investors to get simple exposure to a bunch of non-U.S. equities.

The ETF offers a trailing 3.4% dividend yield, but that yield will likely be higher on a forward basis after the reconstitution and as the underlying holdings raise their dividends. It also offers diversification away from U.S. equities and dividends in multiple different currencies, even if the payout does eventually get converted to U.S. Dollars for the convenience of American shareholders.

The dirt-cheap expense ratio of 0.08% is lower than most comparable ETFs, too. And the quality factors used to choose the portfolio should be enough to ensure very reasonable total returns. Just like SCHD, SCHY isn't just a dividend play.

Put it all together, and I continue to think it's a nice choice for those looking for additional dividend diversification. I believe that SCHY will return approximately 10% per year over the long-term, with a little extra juice in the short-term as investors continue to slowly move out of U.S. equities and into foreign stocks. My total return target is 15% over the next year, and I rank the stock a buy.

data.title: Central Bancompany: Excess Capital Challenges Growth, Profitability Metrics Exceptional
data.description:
Earlier today, Central Bancompany (CBC) released its Q1 2026 financial results, marking only its second earnings report since first becoming a publicly traded company in November of last year. The profit of $0.46 per share was $0.03 better than expectations, but $0.01 behind the previous quarter. Net income of $111.1 million was $3.5 million better than the previous quarter, but the EPS number was impacted by a small increase in the company's tally of fully diluted shares.

This is the second time that I have written about the company that has a history that dates back almost 125 years, even though it only started trading on the NASDAQ in 2025. In my first analysis on January 1, I rated CBC as a Buy, noting its strong profitability metrics and recent earnings growth. Since that article was published, the stock has produced a total return of 8.5%, more than doubling the gains from the S&P 500 during this time. Just a month after its November IPO, Morgan Stanley gave its stock a rating of "overweight", noting the company's low-cost deposit base, diversified revenue streams, above-average profitability.

After looking through the most recent results from the company, there is still a lot to like about Central Bancompany, and I am holding my Buy rating for now. However, the company has a sizeable portion of excess capital that will likely be a funding source for an acquisition in the year ahead. How CBC ultimately decides to use this capital will ultimately determine its ability to take the next step to continue to grow earnings as it has in previous years.

Company Overview

Headquartered in Missouri's state capital, Jefferson City, Central Bancompany is the holding company for The Central Trust Bank, Central Investment Advisors, Central Trust Company, and Central Mortgage Company. Banking operations are the primary function of the company as it operates 156 branch locations in five states including Colorado, Kansas, Oklahoma, and Florida, in addition to its home state. While CBC has a strong presence in Kansas City and St. Louis, as shown in the slide below, the company also has branch locations in a number of smaller, rural markets in the Midwest.

As of March 31, Central Bancompany controlled assets valued at $20.5 billion. That was actually a slight decrease from the $20.8 billion reported at the end of FY 2025. After allowance for credit losses, net loans held by the bank totaled $11.4 billion. This means only about 55.6% of CBC's assets are tied to its loan portfolio, well below the average for a bank of its size.

Further details about the bank's loan portfolio can be seen in the slide below. Gross loans held for investment increased 0.9% from Q4 2025. The largest allocations belong to commercial real estate and home mortgages. CRE makes up 42% of the loan mix with investor CRE holding about twice the allocation of owner-occupied CRE, 28% to 14%. Residential home loans made up 30% of the portfolio at the end of the quarter, and this loan type has shown a 10% increase when compared to Q1 2025. Construction and industrial loans are the only other loan segment that has a double-digit allocation at 15%.

The second largest asset line item on the company's balance sheet, after loans are considered, belongs to $6.8 billion in investment securities. Central Bancompany also holds an additional $1.2 billion in short-term earning assets and $190.9 million in cash. All totaled, CBC states that it is holding $1.9 billion in excess capital at the end of Q1, which would translate into about $7.80 per share, almost half of the company's book value of $15.81 per share. This excess capital is causing the company's Common Equity Tier 1 ratio, 28.6%, to be much higher than its target of 13.5%.

To its credit, the company is doing more to deploy some of this excess to shareholders. CBC repurchased about $32 million of common stock in Q1. It also raised its quarterly dividend 118% from the same period last year to its current level of $0.12 per share. This gives its stock a forward yield of 1.83%. Seeking Alpha currently shows a warning that the company is at risk of cutting its dividend, but the data being used for that assessment is being impacted by Central Bancompany's short history as a public company. Paying a $0.12 dividend on quarterly earnings of $0.46 is quite manageable for the company, especially given the excess capital on its books.

Analysis of Central Bancompany

One of the things that initially attracted me to Central Bancompany was its earnings momentum over the last couple of years. After finishing FY 2024 with EPS of $1.39, the company recorded a FY 2025 profit of $1.75, an improvement of 25.9%. Earnings growth is expected to slow down this year, however. After reporting $0.46 per share in earnings in Q1 this year, the company is on pace to reach $1.87 in FY 2026, an increase of just 6.9%. The cash and short-term assets on the books for CBC simply are not able to earn the same rate of return as they would if they were deployed in other investments.

The good news for the bank is that almost every other aspect of its operations is running well ahead of what would normally be seen from a regional bank with similar total assets. Net interest margin stood at 4.23% at the end of Q1. The bank's efficiency ratio finished the quarter at 46.3%. Return on assets was 2.20%, and return on average equity was 11.8%. The NIM was impacted by the lower returns from the company's non-loan assets, as CBC still has a very low cost of deposits of 1.13%. ROA was three basis points higher than Q4 2025. ROE did take a step back from the previous quarter's 12.1%, again because of the bank's high capital position.

Central Bancompany's cost of deposits is aided by the company's high ratio of non-interest-bearing deposits. Total deposits stood at $15.5 billion at the end of Q1 with $5.6 billion, or 36.0%, held in non-interest-bearing accounts. Those non-interest-bearing balances have grown by roughly 8.6%, year-over-year, making them the bank's fastest growing deposit type. Time deposits make up only 10.5% of the total deposits for CBC.

The final piece of the picture for Central Bancompany is a healthy loan portfolio that has been able to maintain a consistently low level of net charge-offs over the last year. In Q1 2026, net charge-offs were just 0.10%, even with the levels from Q4 2025. CBC's ratio of nonperforming assets was just 0.27% in the last three months, two basis points higher than the previous quarter but one lower than Q1 2025. While delinquent loans stayed relatively low in Q1 as well, the amount of delinquent commercial loans did rise 62% from $10.0 million to $16.2 million. The delinquency ratio of 0.39% is still well below average, however.

Valuation Check-In

Seeking Alpha shows CBC currently trades with a forward PE of 14.29. This is significantly above the mean for the financial sector, and it is one reason why the stock receives an "F" in valuation from Seeking Alpha's quant rating. However, the company's excess capital is once again impacting the true picture here. If you factor out the excess capital which is held by the holding company, the PE multiple for just the bank and its normal operations would be about 10 for the most recent quarter, a much more compelling argument for a potential new investment in the company.

Tangible book value for the company was $14.38 per share at the end of Q1. One share of CBC closed at $25.89 on April 28, which means its most current price to TBV ratio is 1.80. This does make the TBV valuation for Central Bancompany higher than the most recent average for a US bank according to S&P Global, 1.37. However, I would argue that there are very few regional banks in the US with CBC's profitability metrics and strong capital position. The stock is trading at a premium, but for now, the results seem to justify it.

Risks to Consider

Even though the operations of the company look incredibly strong after the first three months of this year, I actually see a few more risks for investing in CBC than I did when I wrote my last analysis. As with any regional bank, Central Bancompany is sensitive to changes in macroeconomic factors like changes in interest rates, elevated inflation, and higher unemployment. Lower rates could actually impact CBC more than other peer banks as the company would not be able to earn as much interest on its cash and short-term securities.

With over 70% of the bank's total loans allocated to some form of real estate, Central Bancompany is also subject to added risks of events that could cause repayment of those loans to move off schedule. A slowdown in business activity in its service areas in the Midwest, especially in its home state of Missouri, could cause a decline in the valuation for the properties used as collateral for those loans. Declines in investor CRE occupancy could also pressure repayment of those loans and harm the previously mentioned healthy asset quality ratios.

The biggest question facing Central Bancompany in the next few quarters is a decision on how it will deploy the excess capital currently on its balance sheet. One or more acquisitions is certainly an outcome with high probability, but the company should avoid temptation to overpay for assets from another bank. Making a deal when there is pressure to do so may not result in the best outcome for shareholders. On the other hand, failure to complete any transactions will challenge the company's ability to continue to grow earnings and expand its return on equity.

The Bottom Line

My optimism for the stock is not quite as high as it was four months ago because of these risks mentioned above. An investment in CBC requires a bit of trust that management will be able to pull the right levers to move the company in the right direction. If there is one final piece of evidence that helps me stay positive for the company's future outlook it is the insider buying that has taken place since the IPO last November. The list of insider transactions below shows 14 different insiders buying with most committing at least $50,000 or more in total.

I am maintaining my Buy rating for Central Bancompany at this time. Its strong profitability metrics and solid asset quality are hard to ignore. There were 70 bank acquisitions announced in the Midwest in 2025, a 52.2% increase from the previous year. It will be interesting to see how CBC may contribute to that total for 2026.

data.title: Midea: A List Of Opportunities Meets An Attractive Valuation
data.description:
Midea Group (OTC:MGCOF) is a Chinese manufacturing conglomerate known for being one of the largest home appliance manufacturers. On top of that, it is also a leading producer of air conditioners and industrial robots. Midea is also engaged in several other high-growth applications and it is growing rapidly in international markets.

Midea’s competitiveness is reflected in strong margins, return on equity and continuous growth. Its valuation appears attractive considering its robust balance sheet, strong track record and numerous opportunities. Over the past years Midea’s business has been highly cash generative. Considering the quality of the business Midea’s shares are attractively valued. Returns are supported by high dividend yield and share buybacks.

Seven-armed industrial robot

Midea is a fairly recent constituent of CSI 300 and Hang Seng indexes that follow companies listed in Chinese mainland and Hong Kong. It has a market capitalization of approximately $86 billion. Midea’s businesses and operations span wide and deep. Geographically, around 57% of the revenue comes from mainland China. It has 65 manufacturing sites, of which 43 are outside of China. The company employs nearly two hundred thousand people.

Midea has seven business units. Smart Home segment represents over 65% of the revenue and consists of home appliances, air conditioners and domestic water systems. By volume Midea is the largest manufacturer of residential air conditioners. Intelligent Building Technology is a collection of companies engaged in commercial heating and cooling solutions and elevators.

Industrial Technology is a division behind the manufacturing of components such as compressors, motors, chips and motion control. Robotics and Automation includes German industrial robotics brand KUKA that was acquired fully in 2022. Smart Logistics, also called ANNTO, is a provider of warehouse automation. Last but not least, Midea Healthcare and Midea Energy are its newest divisions.

Midea operates under multiple brands. In home appliances it has several different brands, such as Midea, COLMO and Toshiba, for different price tiers. It also produces appliances for under brands owned by other manufacturers. An important part of Midea’s philosophy is vertical integration. A key part of many of its products is a compressor. Midea’s subsidiary GMCC Welling is the largest manufacturer of compressors for air conditioning and the second largest for refrigerators. Vertical integration provides Midea a cost advantage over many competitors.

Midea reported its 2025 results at the end of March. Its revenue grew 12% and net income climbed 14%. Earnings per share grew only 7% due to the secondary listing in Hong Kong. Results were also burdened by integration costs of three acquisitions. Intelligent Building Technology grew the strongest with a growth rate of 26%. Robotics and Automation and Industrial Technology grew by 8% and 10%.

Thesis includes a plenty of optionality

The greatest growth driver for Midea is its international expansion. Midea’s manufacturing and sales presence nearly doubled from 2024 to 2025. In 2025, its international sales grew 16%. An important part of the growth is a transition from manufacturing products for others to building sales of its own brands. In 2025, 45% of international sales was its own brands and the company targets 50% in 2026. It has 22 R&D centers to localize products to international markets. Last year, Midea acquired European brand Teka to boost its home appliance sales in the region.

On the 23rd of April, Midea and Electrolux announced a partnership in regards to the North American market and home appliances. The two companies will form joint ventures for manufacturing and sales in order to combine Electrolux’s market position and Midea’s manufacturing expertise. The partnership is seeking to improve cost competitiveness, product portfolio and deliver profitable growth. The news showcases Midea’s position as an industry leader and likely supports its growth in the U.S. market.

Only about 10% of people living in hotter climates have access to cooling. Urbanisation and higher living standards drive demand for air conditioners. More than 40% of Midea's revenue comes from HVAC-products meaning that Midea is riding a megatrend for years to come. In China, commercial HVAC has been largely in the hands of international players. Now, domestic companies, Midea among them, are taking market share.

In Europe, Midea has created a significant presence in the HVAC-industry by acquisitions. Its portfolio includes 15 brands that generate over €1 billion of revenue. The different companies manufacture everything from radiators to large air handling units. In 2025, Midea acquired HVAC-business from Switzerland-based Arbonia. Acquisitions are likely giving Midea a good access to European distribution networks. Especially in Europe, Midea is benefiting from the transition from gas boilers to heat pumps, which drives the demand for its core products.

Midea is engaged in multiple growth sectors and opportunities. Its core appliance business could benefit from IoT-trend. It has 43 million monthly online devices and 20 million active users. As everyone knows, datacenters are a large growth opportunity for cooling equipment companies. Midea has designed datacenter specific products and supplied equipment to companies such as Huawei and Alibaba. It can offer both air and liquid based cooling systems, but it is specifically focusing on the latter one by launching new products and investing in production capacity in China.

As a conglomerate, Midea has a lot of optionality baked in. For example, Midea is developing humanoid robots that are deployed in one of its Chinese factories. Midea is one of the 100 companies listed by Morgan Stanley as humanoid enablers. The combination of Kuka’s robotics expertise and Midea’s expertise in product development, components and manufacturing provide a solid basis to compete in the emerging industry. Meanwhile, its subsidiaries, such as Welling, produce components for robots. The Robotics and Automation division already does approximately $4.5 billion of revenue annually. In 2025, Midea introduced the first six-armed industrial humanoid robot, which showcases Midea’s ability to innovate.

Through its separately listed subsidiaries, Hiconics and Clou, Midea is tapping into photovoltaic energy solutions, energy storage and other products required in the green transition. In December, Midea acquired the non-U.S. business of Carestream Health, a provider of medical imaging, to strengthen its Midea Health division. In addition to CT, MRI and X-ray imaging, the division provides biomedical refrigeration, surgical robotics and pneumatic tube transport systems (Swisslog). Midea announced a RMB 60 billion investment program over the next three years focusing on humanoid robots, AI-integration, green energy solutions and healthcare.

Midea owns 74% of ANNTO, Smart Logistics business, which provides end-to-end logistic services for thousands of customers primarily in China and South-East Asia. In addition, ANNTO is Midea’s logistics arm for its own products to enable direct-to-consumer sales. Midea is in the process of spinning-off ANNTO and listing the shares in Hong Kong. A spin-off could be a small step to reduce Midea's complexity and unlock some value and capital.

Midea’s numerous businesses and growth desires are supported by a robust net-cash balance sheet. In 2025, its cash balance declined from RMB 140 billion down to RMB 85 billion. On the other hand, short-term financial investments increased from RMB 49 billion to RMB 156 billion. In total, Midea carries approximately RMB 300 billion of cash and financial investments on its balance sheet compared to RMB 65 billion of short and long-term debt and RMB 60 billion (NET) accounts payables.

Home appliances have been supported by subsidies

Midea’s primary listing is in China, meaning investors should consider especially geopolitical risks involved when investing in Chinese companies. Furthermore, in most of its markets residential and commercial construction is sluggish, which impacts its appliance and HVAC sales. A sign of recovery would be beneficial for Midea’s stock and benefit a market leader greatly.

Midea’s profitability has climbed back to pandemic highs. Tariffs and higher logistic costs might be a powerful counter force. With its global manufacturing base and the new partnership with Electrolux, Midea could be able to mitigate some of the tariff impacts. In 2025, Midea benefitted from Chinese trade-in financial subsidies from the state to consumers incentivising them to renew their home appliances.

The home appliance sector is highly competitive, although the five largest manufacturers hold a meaningful share of the total market. Midea holds approximately 5% of global market share.It is likely perceived as a home appliance business. Midea is a large enterprise. Its emerging businesses are individually too small to move a needle for Midea. More than one would need to fly in order to impact Midea’s growth rate.

Optionality meets fair valuation

Analysts are expecting steady 6%-7% revenue growth and a little faster earnings growth. Midea is currently trading at a forward P/E-multiple of 13x, close to its historical averages. As can be seen below in the table, Midea is valued more like its home appliance peers. HVAC-companies in the west typically command much higher multiples. Counting in Midea’s RMB 175 billion net cash and financial investment position, P/E-multiple would be around 9.2x. Midea has typically generated more free cash flow than net income. Last year, the free cash flow landed close to 5-year average. Net income was slightly higher than free cash flow and P/FCF-multiple stands at 14.3x.

Midea stands out among its selected peers and competitors. It has better growth and profitability metrics. Haier has similarly an attractive valuation in the same regard. However, its growth rate slowed down in 2025. Haier grew its revenue by 5.7% and earnings per share by 5%. Whirlpool, Electrolux and Carrier are heavily indebted in comparison to Midea. Carrier is a solid business in the HVAC-sector and enjoys high valuation that the sector has attracted. As said, if Midea continues its profitable growth in the business lines outside home appliances the shares could benefit from multiple expansion. Midea’s Intelligent Building Technology division delivered a similar EBITDA-margin as Carrier in 2025.
